tasks.title: Fix population scaling to be proportional to building count
tasks.description: Currently, settlement generation can produce far more buildings than people (e.g., 30 residences, 10 businesses, but only 15 inhabitants). The population should be proportional to buildings. (1) In world-generator.ts and genealogy-generator.ts, add a population target calculation based on building count. (2) Formula: target_population = (num_residences * 4.5) + (num_businesses * 2.5). The residence multiplier assumes ~4-5 people per household. The business multiplier accounts for owners and at least 1 employee. (3) After geography generation determines the number of residences and businesses, pass the target population to the genealogy generator. (4) In genealogy-generator.ts, adjust the number of founding families and generations to produce approximately the target population of living characters. (5) Add a minimum population floor: at least enough people to have 1 owner per business + 2 residents per residence. (6) Add a sanity check after generation: if population < minimum_floor, generate additional individual characters (non-family immigrants) to fill the gap. (7) Log the final ratio: 'Settlement generated: X people, Y residences, Z businesses (ratio: A people/residence, B people/business)'.

tasks.title: Ensure every business has an owner assigned during generation
tasks.description: Every procedurally generated business must have an owner. (1) In world-generator.ts, after characters and businesses are generated, add a business owner assignment phase. (2) For each business that lacks an ownerId (or has ownerId pointing to a non-existent/dead character), assign an owner from the available population. (3) Owner selection priority: characters with matching skills/occupation for the business type (e.g., a character with cooking skills for a Restaurant), then characters without any current business ownership, then any available adult character. (4) If there are more businesses than available adults, generate additional characters specifically to be business owners. (5) Set the business.ownerId and business.founderId to the assigned character. (6) Update the character's occupation/employment data to reflect business ownership. (7) Assert the Prolog fact owns(characterId, businessId) for each assignment. (8) Add validation: after the assignment phase, verify every business has a valid, living owner.

tasks.title: Ensure every business has employees assigned during generation
tasks.description: Businesses should have employees in addition to owners. (1) In world-generator.ts, after business owners are assigned, run an employee assignment phase. (2) Determine employee count per business based on type: small businesses (Bakery, Barbershop, Tailor) get 1-2 employees, medium businesses (Restaurant, Shop, Hotel) get 2-4 employees, large businesses (Factory, Hospital, University) get 4-8 employees. (3) Use the hiring-system.ts evaluateCandidate() logic to find qualified candidates from the unemployed population. (4) Assign employees to businesses, updating their occupation records. (5) Fill business vacancies using the fillVacancy() function. (6) If there aren't enough unemployed characters to staff all businesses, generate additional immigrant characters. (7) After assignment, every business should have at minimum: 1 owner + 1 employee. (8) Store employee IDs in a queryable format so the admin panel can display them.

tasks.title: Ensure every residence has owners and residents assigned during generation
tasks.description: Every procedurally generated residence should have owners and residents. Currently all residences show Owners (0) and Residents (0). (1) In world-generator.ts, review the residence assignment code at lines 827-872. (2) Fix the assignment logic: group family members together and assign each family to a residence. Nuclear families (parents + children) should share a residence. (3) The first adult in each family assigned to a residence should also be added to ownerIds. (4) Ensure residentIds is properly populated with ALL family members living in the residence. (5) After family assignment, if residences remain empty, assign remaining unhoused characters (immigrants, orphans, single adults) to available residences. (6) Verify the assignment is persisted correctly: after calling storage.updateResidence(), confirm ownerIds and residentIds are saved to the database. (7) Add validation: after assignment, log how many residences are occupied vs. empty, and flag if any character is unhoused.

tasks.title: Show business owners and employees in Society Settlement editor
tasks.description: The Society Settlement editor does not show business owners or employees. (1) In SettlementDetailView.tsx (or the relevant settlement editor component), find the business listing section. (2) For each business card/row, display: Business name and type, Owner name (resolved from ownerId via character lookup), Employee list (query occupations/employment records for this businessId and resolve character names). (3) Add an 'Employees' subsection under each business showing each employee's name and role/position. (4) If a business has no owner, show a warning: 'No owner assigned'. (5) If a business has no employees, show 'No employees'. (6) Make owner and employee names clickable to navigate to the character detail view. (7) Show employee count as a badge on the business card: 'Staff: X'.

tasks.title: Show residence owners and residents in Society Settlement editor
tasks.description: Fix the settlement editor to correctly display residence occupancy. (1) In SettlementDetailView.tsx, find the residence listing section where it currently shows 'Owners (0)' and 'Residents (0)'. (2) Debug why ownerIds and residentIds appear empty: check if the data is missing from the database, if the API response doesn't include these fields, or if the frontend is not reading them correctly. (3) Ensure the API endpoint that returns residences includes the full ownerIds and residentIds arrays. (4) For each residence, display: Address and type, Owner names (resolved from ownerIds), Resident names (resolved from residentIds) with relationship labels if available (e.g., 'John Smith (father)', 'Jane Smith (mother)', 'Jimmy Smith (son)'). (5) Show occupancy: 'X / Y capacity'. (6) Make resident names clickable to navigate to character details. (7) Color-code: green for occupied, yellow for partially occupied, red for empty.

tasks.title: Populate businesses with NPCs during game loading
tasks.description: Businesses in the game world are currently empty with no NPCs inside, making interiors lifeless. (1) In BabylonGame.ts or the NPC spawning system, when loading a settlement's businesses, spawn NPC models for: the business owner (always present during business hours), employees (present during their work shifts), and occasional customers (1-3 random NPCs from the settlement population). (2) Position the owner NPC behind a counter or at a desk appropriate to the business type. (3) Position employees at their work stations (e.g., cook in kitchen, clerk at shelf, guard at door). (4) Position customer NPCs in the public/shopping areas of the business. (5) Give NPCs idle animations appropriate to their role: owner manning the counter, employees working, customers browsing. (6) NPCs in businesses should be interactable: clicking on the owner/employees should allow trading or dialogue.

tasks.title: Implement day/night NPC location cycling
tasks.description: Business owners and employees should go to work during the day and return home at night. (1) Implement a time-of-day system that tracks game hours (if not already present). (2) Define work hours per business type: most businesses 8am-6pm, bars/restaurants 10am-11pm, etc. (3) During work hours: business owners and employees should be located at their business interior. Spawn their NPC models inside the business. (4) During off hours: owners and employees should return to their residences. Move their NPC models to their assigned residence interior or despawn from business and spawn at residence. (5) Transition: at opening time, NPCs walk from residence to business (or teleport if the walk system isn't implemented). At closing time, they walk/teleport back. (6) Customers should appear at businesses during business hours with varying frequency (more at midday, fewer early/late). (7) At night, most NPCs should be at home. A few night-owl NPCs can be at bars or wandering. (8) Update the minimap NPC markers to reflect current NPC positions based on time of day.

tasks.title: Map assets to all base items that show 'No asset mapped for role'
tasks.description: The vast majority of Base Items have 'No asset mapped for role', preventing them from being rendered in the world. (1) In ItemsHub.tsx, identify the full list of objectRole values that have no mapped asset by querying all items and checking which roles lack entries in the asset collection's objectModels. (2) Catalog all available item/object models in the asset collection (public/assets/models/, public/assets/objects/, and any other model directories). (3) Create a mapping from objectRole to the best-matching available model. Use approximate matching: 'sword' role → any sword model, 'bread' role → any food/bread model, 'potion' role → any bottle model, etc. (4) For roles with no good match, assign a reasonable generic model: weapons → a generic weapon model, tools → a generic tool, food → a generic food item, clothing → a generic clothing item, etc. (5) Store these mappings in the asset collection configuration. (6) Verify every objectRole has some asset mapped. (7) Create a report of which mappings are approximate/placeholder so they can be refined later.

tasks.title: Populate items throughout building interiors and exteriors
tasks.description: Items should be visually present throughout the game world, especially in interiors. (1) In item-placement-generator.ts, enhance the item placement logic to place more items in more locations. (2) Business interiors should have items appropriate to their type: restaurants have plates/cups/food on tables, shops have merchandise on shelves, blacksmiths have weapons/tools on racks, bakeries have bread/pastries on display, banks have coins/ledgers. (3) Residence interiors should have household items: plates, cups, books, clothing, tools, decorative items placed on furniture surfaces. (4) Exterior lots should occasionally have items: crates near businesses, tools near workshops, produce near farms, barrels near taverns. (5) For each placed item, assign a world position (x, y, z) relative to the building interior or lot. (6) In the 3D game, load and render item models at their assigned positions using the asset mappings from the previous task. (7) Make placed items interactable: the player can examine, pick up, or purchase them depending on context.

tasks.title: Populate seller inventories with appropriate items for sale
tasks.description: Businesses that sell items should have populated inventories. (1) Define which business types are sellers and what categories of items they sell: Shop/GroceryStore (general goods, food), Bakery (baked goods, flour, ingredients), Blacksmith (weapons, tools, metal items), JewelryStore (jewelry, gems), BookStore (books, scrolls, paper), HerbShop (herbs, potions, ingredients), PawnShop (misc items, used goods), Tailor (clothing, fabric), Brewery (drinks, barrels). (2) In the world generator or a new inventory-generator, when a seller business is created, populate its inventory with 10-30 items appropriate to its type. (3) Pull items from the base items table filtered by relevant tags/categories. (4) Set prices for inventory items based on the item's base value with a merchant markup (1.2x-2x). (5) Display seller inventory when the player interacts with the business owner: show a shop UI with item names, icons (from asset mappings), prices, and buy button. (6) Visually display some inventory items on shelves/counters in the business interior.
